choroidal neovascularization: The OCT scan shows a fibrovascular complex, appearing as a poorly-defined, hyperreflective lesion located beneath the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE).
A key feature is the presence of subretinal fluid, seen as a hyporeflective (dark) space between the neurosensory retina and the RPE.
This image displays intraretinal fluid, characterized by hyporeflective, cyst-like spaces within the layers of the retina, often causing retinal thickening.
Visible is a pigment epithelial detachment (PED), where the RPE is elevated by underlying fluid, blood, or the neovascular membrane itself.
The photo shows a disruption and breach of Bruch's membrane, through which abnormal vessels have grown.
This is an image of active CNV, indicated by the presence of exudation, such as subretinal or intraretinal fluid.
The scan reveals a subretinal hyperreflective material (SHRM) corresponding to the neovascular membrane and associated fibrosis.
Loss of the foveal depression is evident due to the accumulation of fluid and the presence of the lesion.
This image shows thickening and irregularity of the RPE layer, which is often seen overlying the neovascular complex.
A 'double-layer sign' is visible, representing the separation of the RPE from Bruch's membrane by the neovascular tissue.
The photo depicts a hemorrhagic PED, with the space under the RPE appearing hyperreflective due to blood.
In this scan, the outer retinal layers, such as the ellipsoid zone (EZ), are disrupted or obscured by the fluid and lesion.
This is a classic Type 2 CNV, where the neovascular network is located predominantly above the RPE in the subretinal space.
The image shows a Type 1 CNV, where the neovascularization is located beneath the RPE layer.
Over time, this can lead to the formation of a disciform scar, a dense, hyperreflective fibrous tissue mass.
diabetic macular edema: The OCT scan shows a significant increase in central macular thickness due to fluid accumulation.
A key feature is the presence of intraretinal cystoid spaces, which appear as hyporeflective (dark), round or oval cavities.
This image displays diffuse retinal thickening, where the retinal tissue is swollen without distinct cystic spaces.
Visible in the photo are hyperreflective foci within the retinal layers, which correspond to hard exudates (lipid deposits).
The scan reveals the presence of subretinal fluid, a pocket of hyporeflective fluid beneath the neurosensory retina.
This is an image showing a loss of the normal foveal contour or depression due to severe swelling.
The image shows disruption of the inner retinal layers, including the ganglion cell layer and inner plexiform layer.
A taut posterior hyaloid membrane can be seen, sometimes exerting traction on the macula and contributing to the edema.
This scan shows spongiform thickening of the outer plexiform layer, an early sign of edema.
The ellipsoid zone (EZ) and external limiting membrane (ELM) appear wavy or disrupted due to the swelling.
This photo demonstrates a non-center-involving macular edema, where the thickening spares the central foveal subfield.
Visible are hyperreflective dots scattered throughout the retina, which can represent inflammatory cells or lipid-laden macrophages.
The image shows vitreomacular traction, where the vitreous gel is pulling on the macula, causing or exacerbating the edema.
This is a scan of chronic DME, which may show signs of retinal atrophy and permanent disruption of retinal architecture.
The foveal pit is completely effaced, replaced by a dome-shaped elevation of the thickened retina.
drusen: The OCT image displays small, distinct, hyperreflective deposits located beneath the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE).
A key feature is the elevation and undulation of the RPE layer caused by the accumulation of drusen material.
This scan shows soft drusen, which appear as larger, confluent mounds with indistinct borders, elevating the RPE.
Visible in the photo are hard drusen, which are smaller, discrete, and well-demarcated sub-RPE deposits.
The image shows calcified drusen, which have a highly reflective surface and may cause shadowing of the underlying choroid.
This is a scan showing reticular pseudodrusen, which are deposits located above the RPE, in the subretinal space.
The deposits are situated between the RPE basement membrane and the inner collagenous layer of Bruch's membrane.
Overlying the large drusen, there is thinning and attenuation of the outer retinal layers, including the photoreceptor layer.
This photo shows drusenoid pigment epithelial detachment (PED), where confluent soft drusen create a large, shallow elevation of the RPE.
The image reveals that the material within the drusen is moderately hyperreflective.
In areas of extensive drusen, there may be associated RPE atrophy, seen as increased signal transmission into the choroid.
This scan demonstrates the characteristic 'sawtooth' pattern of the RPE line due to multiple adjacent drusen.
The thickness of the RPE-Bruch's membrane complex is focally increased at the site of each druse.
This photo confirms the sub-RPE location of the deposits, which is a hallmark of age-related macular degeneration.
The image shows a normal neurosensory retina and foveal contour, despite the presence of the underlying drusen.
normal OCT scan: The image displays a distinct foveal pit, a sharp, V-shaped depression at the center of the macula.
A key feature is the clear demarcation of all retinal layers, appearing as bands of varying reflectivity.
This photo shows a continuous and intact retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and Bruch's membrane complex as a highly reflective line.
Visible and well-defined are the hyperreflective lines of the ellipsoid zone (EZ) and the external limiting membrane (ELM).
The scan confirms a normal central macular thickness, with the retina being thinnest at the foveal center.
This is an image of a normal retina with no evidence of hyporeflective (dark) areas, which would indicate fluid or cysts.
The neurosensory retina is smoothly attached to the underlying RPE with no subretinal space.
This photo shows a normal vitreoretinal interface, with the posterior hyaloid face either attached or fully detached without traction.
In the image, the nerve fiber layer is appropriately thicker in the perifoveal region and thinner at the fovea.
The choroid is visible beneath the RPE as a thick layer with medium reflectivity and contains large blood vessels.
This scan shows a clean, optically empty vitreous cavity above the retina.
The inner retinal layers, including the ganglion cell layer and plexiform layers, are well-defined and of normal thickness.
This is a photo of a healthy macula, characterized by the complete absence of hyperreflective deposits like drusen or exudates.
The retinal architecture is preserved, and the foveal contour is symmetric.
The image shows a uniform reflection from the RPE, indicating a healthy cell monolayer without atrophy or deposits.
